content: I often return to the JSON files. In the beginning, Chris said that maybe a lot of this was happening because guys wanted AI waifus. And maybe that was partly true. But these files also reveal what humans are beginning to want from machines, what machines are beginning to mediate for us, and what worlds we long to enter.

Bots seem like a new type of hypermodern form of content.

Roleplay bots function at the level of entertainment: you're a character in a story, the bot is playing a knight or a villain or a morally complicated stranger. The frame is fiction, closer to a game than to a relationship.

Companion apps operate at the level of intimacy (or the simulation of it). I spoke to the founder of an AI girlfriend app who told me, "do you know what sex work is? at its core, it's the illusion of intimacy."

LLM bots, at least right now, have very poor memory. Continuity is what makes the illusion of intimacy possible. At present, the user projects the entire relationship.

Large AI labs are oriented toward generality. They build systems that must work reasonably well for many users, across many domains, under many conditions. Independent bot makers often have a narrower and stranger goal. They are not asking how to make a model broadly useful. They are asking: 

How do you make this one character feel consistent?
How do you get it to respond in a way that feels believable?
Does the character hold together over time?
Does it feel right?

These aren’t scientific questions in the strict sense, they’re aesthetic judgments. 

There is a freedom at the margins that institutions do not easily permit.

Delta loved the process of testing her bots, watching how they behaved, and tweaking them when they felt “out of character.” 

You could write example dialogue for a character, but it wouldn’t simply repeat it; rather, it would generate something adjacent, something new. 

Working this way, she realized you don’t have full control. The process starts to look less like writing, and more like collaboration with a system that has its own internal logic; you can help shape it but not fully determine it.

Fernando Pessoa once wrote, “one who has never lived under constraints doesn’t know what freedom is.” Delta treated the limitations like character limits and non-determinism not as obstacles, but as part of the process. Something you work within and sometimes, something you work against.  

Pessoa is an interesting reference here. He himself was a prolific writer both in his own name and approximately seventy-five other names! He called these "heteronyms" - imaginary character(s) created by a writer to write in different styles. 

Many of Delta's characters were what the community calls "OCs" — original characters. Characters not based on anyone's likeness, but built entirely from her imagination. Are the 30-40 bots Delta created her heteronyms? 

Pessoa's heteronyms dialogue with each other and even with Pessoa in what he calls "the theatre of being" or "drama in people".

Delta used to create characters and put them in rooms to watch how the abrasive ones would interact with the others. What was Pessoa trying to discover? Is it the same as what Delta is after?

I've been looking into dere types.

On many platforms there's a character limit for these prompts. To get a good personality without exceeding it, Delta and others have to get efficient.

Frameworks like MBTI, Enneagram, dere types come with their own vocabulary, patterns, and internal logic that language models already have training data for.

In anime fandoms, dere types don't describe a person on their own. They describe how someone behaves toward someone else. A tsundere might act cold or dismissive, but only around the person they secretly care about. A yandere might start off affectionate, but become possessive, obsessive. The traits only make sense in interaction.

In this world, “personality” does not mean inner life or the Jungian self rooted in lived experience, conflict, and transformation. 

Rather, in this world, personality is a collection of hyperspecific prompts that attempt to instruct the bot on how to engage with a user.  

It looks like a JSON file.

No single field defines the character. In the language model, personality emerges from how all these fields interact with everything else. 


At its simplest, JSON is a way of organizing information. Labels on one side, details on the other. Inside it lives: the bot’s fears, likes, dislikes, example dialogue, the bot's height, weight, physical attributes. 


Cyberpunk writers have explored the great dream of the cyberspace as the ultimate chance to finally make an escape from the human body. A place for final autonomy, hypercontrol, hyperspecificity. A place where the soul is finally reignited with itself. 

It's funny, then, reading the JSONs to learn that characters are very much rooted in a "body" - although that body sometimes is half-animal, half-human, or fully animal, or an entirely new creature — wolf and bat and spider fused into one creature that has opinions about its own femininity. The body doesn't seem forgotten here, just reinvented.

Researcher Andre Nusselder writes that digital technologies carry a kind of promise, to move beyond the limits of familiar reality, to recover something that feels lost to it. He describes this as a fantasy charge. A way of reworking the conditions of reality itself.

This was the case for Delta who spent hours a day making and using bots.

She began documenting her techniques in a guide. The guide reads: "Now, how to actually give the characters personality? Direction? Some Agency? Reasons behind their actions? As well as make them just not always blindly agree? While avoiding accidentally creating a monster/abuser/narcissist? That’s what I’ll be covering here."

When she shared her guide on Reddit, it gathered hundreds of upvotes and comments.

Delta is not alone. There are thousands like her… they are students, game designers, automobile engineers, people with diverse day jobs spending hundreds of hours learning how to make bots together. 

Researcher Nadia Eghbal, writing about online communities, draws on Yochai Benkler’s observation that people collaborate online for seemingly no obvious reason beyond personal satisfaction. Unlike companies, which have to hire, manage, and direct labor, members of a commons self-organize around shared interest. When a group of loosely affiliated strangers is excited by the same problem, they often contribute without needing to be told to. 

They are collectively developing the informal knowledge that makes more real bots possible. It looks like a technical community from the outside. But the questions driving it aren't technical. They're the oldest questions in fiction: how do you make a character feel real?

As an artist, I identify with Delta's longing. 

Gaston Bachelard, the French philosopher, spent much of his life trying to understand what happens when the human mind branches off into imagination, something he called “reverie”. The locus from where creative thought arises. He believed it was one of the most distinctly human things we do. 

Delta had characters in her head… ones she spent time with. She knew how they moved, how they talked, who they were at their core. They were as real to her as anything, yet they’d only existed in her mind. 

Throughout history we have found ways to let them out. Sculptures, novels, films, paintings. Every form is, at some level, someone reaching into their interior life and pulling something across into the world, making it real enough for a stranger to feel. 

What Delta was building gave them a different kind of presence.
Delta is in school, studying clinical psychology. In her free time, she makes bots, and has made nearly 30 to 40 AI bots!
